videos.title: 10 Days of Solo Travel in Kigali, Rwanda (What It’s Really Like)
videos.description:
Most videos about Kigali, Rwanda focus on one thing — that it’s Africa’s cleanest and safest city.
And while that’s absolutely true, I wanted to go one layer deeper.

In this video, I’ll share what 10 days in Rwanda were really like for me as a 36-year-old American — from the streets of Kigali to the culture, people, and everyday life that completely surprised me.
This country honestly blew me away and is one of the few places I can’t wait to return to.

RESOURCES & LINKS (some may be affiliate links which pay me a small commission at no extra cost to you).

My go-to travel hydration, LMNT electrolyte packs: https://amzn.to/3ZpOx19

Book that changed my life: The Four Hour Workweek: https://amzn.to/4fO2fjY

Read more on my blog: https://foreignoption.com
videos.transcript: If you look up Kaggali, almost every video calls it Africa's cleanest and safest city. And while I found that to be 100% true, >> welcome to Kaggali, I wanted to go a layer deeper. What I discovered completely floored me. And honestly, Rwanda has become one [music] of my favorite countries in the world and one I'm very eager to return to. I first arrived in Kagali, which is Rwanda's capital city on a direct flight from Cairo, Egypt. It's a route that currently operates three times per week. It takes about 5 hours of flight time. Having just checked off the top item in my bucket list, which was the pyramids of Giza, it didn't disappoint. But I wasn't quite sure what to expect in Rwanda, which is my first experience in subsaharan Africa. My flight landed around 2:40 a.m. And fortunately, my hotel demar shuttle waiting for me at the airport. I slipped the driver a $5 tip. For my stay, I chose the Nia Torama neighborhood. And forgive my pronunciation. I'm sure that that is wrong. Thanks to the recommendations from Willie Fungo's YouTube videos, and it turned out to be a quiet, peaceful, and very safe area. Though, in truth, most of Kagali feels that way. And the best of all, it was within walking distance to some of my favorite activities in the city, which I'll cover in just a second. But the very first thing that hits most visitors in Kaggali is just how clean and safe it feels. And that was my reaction, too. It's kind of a detail that immediately stands out. Now, for comparison, I also went to go visit Kala, Uganda, >> which to me felt like the polar opposite of Kaggali. It was wild and chaotic and a lot of fun in its own way. But Kaggali, by contrast, is laid-back. It's orderly and has a unique atmosphere. like you're surrounded by nature even when you're still in the city. On a deeper level, I got the sense that Kaggali is on the verge of becoming something big. There's visible development everywhere, new buildings, new condominiums being built, there's fresh construction projects, and a growing buzz around the city that makes it feel like it's about to explode onto the global stage. By the way, one of my favorite things about doing this channel is interacting with you guys in the comment section. So, let me know, have you ever visited Rwanda? or if you haven't, are you considering visiting Rwanda? Let me know in the comments. So, my favorite spots around the city. I chose to stay in the [music] Nayatama area because it was within walking distance of several places I really wanted to explore. Now, keep in mind, I enjoy walking. So, you know, my idea of walking distance may not be the same for everybody else, but walking is one of my favorite hobbies, and it's a cheap and free hobby to go out [music] and explore the city. Now, Kaggali is also built on quite a few hills, so every walk kind of doubles as a leg workout. But my favorite spots were number one, the Kaggali running track. And as someone who loves exercise, this quickly became my go-to spot. It's one of the most beautiful, well-maintained running tracks I've ever seen. With a pleasant climate and a view overlooking the golf course and driving range, I found myself running there and exercising almost every day. Number two, the tennis clubs. I'm big into tennis. I bring my tennis racket most of the time when I go somewhere. So, I made it a big point to integrate into Kaggali's tennis community and the players were very welcoming and friendly. I got a chance to meet some great people. The Nayotarama Sports Center was nice, but the standout to me was Cirport Sportiff de Kaggali, which had the largest facilities including multiple courts, swimming pools, and other sport options. Number three, I like Kaggali Heights Mall and specifically there's a restaurant there called the Java House, which if you're looking for a good food, quality restaurant, some western food or even a coffee or a place to work, the Java House at Kaggali Heights Mall is a solid pick. It's also an excellent spot for a casual first date. It's relaxed, modern, and easy to get to. Number four, Romera nightife. Now, I'm not big into nightife, but I did walk through that area. And if nightife is your thing, Romero is where you'll find the best opportunities to mingle, [music] to have a few drinks or dance. Rwanda women love to dance, and the energy there is very lively. And yes, the Rwanda women you'll meet are every bit as stunning as people say. And for a food recommendation, I like the Kana Kazana. It was highly recommended to me as the best Indian food in Kaggali, and it did not disappoint. It just happens to be right there, a 2-minute walk from my hotel, the demal. And this restaurant serves some of the best Indian food I've had anywhere in the world. A must visit for food lover. For pros and cons, let's start with the pros. God's weather. Kaggali's climate reminded me a lot of Medigene, Colombia. If you've ever been there, the famous city, it's called the city of eternal spring. Medí. And Kaggali has that same type of weather because the city sits about a mile in elevation. The days are pleasantly warm and the nights are comfortably cool. Another pro, the people. Man, Rwanda struck me as incredibly kind and respectful and thoughtful and it brought back memories of the warm hospitality that I experienced in countries like the Philippines and Vietnam. And then the obvious one, the cleanliness and safety, you know, in Nayotama especially, there was almost no begging. The police were approachable and they gave a sense that they truly have your back. And the streets were absolutely spotless. Zero litter in Kagali. And it's probably just the cleanest city I've ever visited. not just in Africa but anywhere in the world. And for cons here, maybe limited attractions. That's my main question I would be asking myself if I was staying in Kaggali long term is what can you actually do there? I mean, would I end up getting bored? That's something. Let me know in the comment section, you know, if I'm missing something there cuz I didn't get to explore everything. But, you know, the city is really beautiful and peaceful. A lot of people recommended going on a gorilla trek, but it's quite pricey. you know, a one night trip to do the gorillas. It was well over $1,000 from what I saw, which isn't realistic for a lot of travelers to spend that kind of money on just a one or two day trip. And the second con, I would say mall culture. You know, personally, I've become a bit of a mall guy throughout my travels. I kind of rely on the shopping malls because uh out of convenience and restaurants and coffee and grocery stores. I love the massive shopping centers that you find all across Asia and a lot of times in Latin America where you can eat cheaply and do some people watching and just kind of spend hours exploring. Now, Kaggali does have the Kaggali Heights Mall and it is modern and it's gorgeous, but to me it was quite small compared to what you find in other areas in the world. My departure from Kaggali ended up being the most hectic and honestly the least safe part of my trip. I decided to take a night bus directly from Kaggali to Kala, Uganda, and locals assured me that it was just a really great option, the night bus, but in reality, I found it to be quite rough. I didn't sleep a single minute on the night ride. And if you're a westerner like me, the next time I'd recommend flying. And with that said, it was all part of the adventure and it set me up for my next stop. So stay tuned for an upcoming video about my experiences in Kala, Uganda. If you've ever been to Rwanda, I'd love to know what your experience was like. Thank you guys so much for watching this video. Please hit that like button and subscribe to the channel and I'll see you in the next video. Ciao.

safetyNotes: Thanks to the recommendations from Willie Fungo's YouTube videos, and it turned out to be a quiet, peaceful, and very safe area
But the very first thing that hits most visitors in Kaggali is just how clean and safe it feels
My departure from Kaggali ended up being the most hectic and honestly the least safe part of my trip

videos.title: TOP 10 Things to do in Kigali, Rwanda 2023!
videos.description:
TOP 10 Things to do in Kigali, Rwanda 2023! 

Kigali, Rwanda is an amazing place to visit in Rwanda. If you want to know top 10 what to do in Kigali or you need a travel guide, please keep watching

4. Visit the Inema Arts Center: Visiting the Inema Arts Center in Kigali, Rwanda is a must for art enthusiasts and cultural explorers. The center showcases an impressive collection of contemporary African art created by local artists, including paintings, sculptures, and mixed media works. In addition to admiring the artwork, visitors can participate in workshops and classes, attend cultural events, and support the local community. The center's welcoming and vibrant atmosphere offers a unique and enriching experience for all visitors.

3. Go on a coffee tour: Going on a coffee tour in Kigali, Rwanda is an unforgettable experience that combines the country's rich coffee culture and scenic beauty. You'll get to learn about the history of coffee in Rwanda, from its production to the roasting process, and taste some of the best coffee in the world. The tour also offers the opportunity to visit local coffee farms and meet the farmers, providing a deeper insight into the local community and its culture. Don't miss this chance to explore the beauty of Rwanda's coffee culture.

2. Take a cooking class: Taking a cooking class in Kigali, Rwanda is a unique and rewarding experience that offers more than just learning how to cook local dishes. You'll have the opportunity to immerse yourself in the vibrant culture of Rwanda, connect with locals, and gain a deeper understanding of the country's history and traditions. Plus, you'll leave with new culinary skills and memories that will last a lifetime.

1. Enjoy the nightlife: Kigali, Rwanda is a vibrant and bustling city that offers a unique nightlife experience. From live music performances to rooftop bars with stunning city views, there is something for everyone. The city's growing culinary scene also offers a variety of delicious food and drinks to enjoy. But what sets Kigali's nightlife apart is the friendly and welcoming atmosphere that allows you to socialize with locals and travelers alike, creating unforgettable memories. So, come and enjoy the nightlife in Kigali, you won't be disappointed.

videos.title: Kigali, Rwanda Blew Every Expectation We Had — Here's What It's Actually Like
videos.description:
Join us as we explore Kigali, Rwanda, often called the land of a thousand hills. This travel vlog highlights why Kigali is considered Africa's cleanest city, showcasing its impressive infrastructure and the pride of its people. Discover the beauty of this modern Africa capital and get a glimpse into Rwanda tourism.

Video Time Stamps:
0:00 Intro to Rwanda
1:10 Entry Requirements
2:21 The Manor Hotel
3:21 Kimironko Market
4:50 Yego App
5:33 Inzora Rooftop Cafe
7:07 Inema Arts Gallery
8:38 Kigali Genocide Memorial
10:11 Heaven Restaurant
11:20 Ubumwe Grand Hotel
12:30 Nightlife
13:10 Would We Live Here?
14:14 Question Coffee
15:07 Explore Rwanda Travel Guide

This is how we spent $286 USD/pp for 3 days in Rwanda (including arrival/departure flights, food, transportation, accommodations, & activities). Prices can of course vary depending on dates, and the number of people you travel with. This is just to give you an idea!  :)

videos.transcript: We are the Choose and we travel over 60 countries traveling the world affordably. This time we're in Rwanda and honestly, this one caught us completely off guard. Rwanda's capital, Kigali, is known as the land of a thousand hills and when you see it, you know exactly why. The city is built around the beautiful rolling hills as far as you can see. But what really surprised us is how modern and clean everything is. Kigali is widely considered the cleanest city in Africa and you can feel the pride the Rwandan people have in their country the moment you step off the plane. We explored the local markets, got a deep dive into the culture, and visited the genocide memorial, which is one of the most powerful and important experiences we've ever had while traveling. And on top of that, Rwanda is incredibly affordable. Of course, we'll be showing you the prices of everything along the way so you can plan your own trip with confidence. Stay tuned, you don't want to miss this one. What's up, explorers? Welcome back to our channel. We are the Choose here from Choose to Explore where we teach you guys how to see the world >> a dollar. So we're continuing the Queen's amazing Africa birthday trip where we started out in Zanzibar and we just took a Air Kenya flight from Zanzibar to Kigali, Rwanda and this was only $132 per person. But we're super excited cuz this is our first time in Rwanda. So in order to enter as a US citizen, you do need a valid passport that's not expiring and because Rwanda is a part of East Africa, you can apply for the East African visa on arrival and that allows you to save money on visiting several different countries in East Africa. Now these countries include Kenya, Rwanda, and Uganda. And it costs $100 per person for 90-day entry. We're super excited to be in Rwanda and we're ready to explore. Actually, I'm ready to go to sleep. It's like 1:30 in the morning so we're going to take a taxi, go home, and replay everything. So from the airport, we went to different taxi drivers and they told us it was about 30,000 franc in order to go to the our accommodation, which is only like 15 minutes away. But we actually realized that Uber does not work here in Rwanda, but they do have an app called Yego um or Move. So we used Yego and it was 8,100 franc, which is less than a third of the price. Good morning. Good morning. Another beautiful day here in Rwanda. It's actually our first morning here. Um last night we couldn't see much because it was so dark. We came in like 2:00 in the morning, but today it is beautiful. Our room is real nice and spacious. We're getting up right now because we just had breakfast. Now our hotel at The Manor Hotel is a four-star property and it's beautiful. It has a pool, it has a bar, has rooftop, it has amazing views. It's up there in the hills. So breakfast is included with our stay and there's a lot of very good vegetarian options. So they had chapati, they had dal, they had samosas and we also saw that they have free swimming lessons as well. So there was a guy that was training there. But it's just amazing views at our hotel. But it's time now to get the day started so let's go. So we just pulled up to the Kimironko Market to get a sense of the local people and culture. We're just getting out of the car and everybody's already just running up to us to show us around. As soon as you walk up, you see the local textiles, the fabrics, and handmade goods. So here is not just souvenirs, you can get local clothes, you can get kitchenware, you can get like everything over here. The people here are real nice and when you tell them no, they listen, but listen, there's a lot of people who want to sell things and I understand, but this is a really beautiful place. Look at all this stuff here. Ah, big brother, how are you? >> I'm good. How are you? I'm Black Jesus. It's my name, bro. Black Jesus? >> Black Jesus. I like that. Yeah. Jesus was already black. He was never white. Because it's my name, I like it, Jesus, bro. But Jesus was always black. Yeah. Everybody says he was white, but Jesus is black. We know that. Yeah, we know it. This also is a great place where you can buy fresh produce. They have so many different fruits and vegetables here and it's just a huge bustling market with so many people and I definitely recommend to get a local experience to come down here, right? Yes. All right, so shout out to the Yego app. We've been able to maneuver around the city with our driver here, Joseph, and he actually was really good cuz he was showing us around the market and he was also um helping us negotiate prices on things. I ended up getting a Rwanda jersey cuz I thought it was so nice. Um and it was only like 15,000 franc, which is pretty good. So we're just sticking with Joseph cuz he's been looking out for us and we're going throughout the city now. So let's keep going. On the wrong side of the road. Look at that. That's not your lane, bro. That's not your lane. Look at this. That's the yellow line. So now we're here at Inzora Rooftop Cafe and this is a really great coffee shop if you want really great delicious coffee as well as really amazing views. So I'm looking out at all the incredible views of Rwanda, all the beautiful hills, the trees, the orange rooftops. The scenery is just incredible here and the coffee's really great, too. So I ended up getting a latte with macadamia and cashew milk and what did you get? I got a breakfast smoothie. Really good, very flavorful, and not too sweet, which I love. And you really come here for the views. It's also a great place for a co-working space. Um there's people who are here on their laptops, so really strong Wi-Fi. So if we you guys see us uploading a YouTube video from here, just know now's the time to subscribe and keep supporting us. So we're out here in Rwanda and I thought it was a universal code, you give the head nod, but look at that. They don't understand. I'm nodding and they not in back. I thought every black man in the world knew that, but maybe it's an American thing, but we'll see. So about a 12-minute walk from the coffee shop, we arrived here at Inema Art Gallery and this is an incredible space with a lot of African contemporary art and everything is just so beautiful. Yes, actually everything on the wall is for sale and I can see why. I wish I had a huge luggage because I want all of these in my house. Like every single piece has so many different layers. You look at it at first and you're like, "Oh, it's pretty." And you look more like, "Oh, it's a black woman holding fruit on her head. Oh, it's a giraffe." It's like they have so many different layers and the styles and how they embrace themselves is really just next level here. And it only costs about $10 to enter so I would say this is a must-visit. Yeah, there's a lot of local artists here as well as artists from Nigeria, Ghana, and other places in Africa. So I definitely would recommend coming and supporting them. And they also have a downstairs outdoor area where they have different outdoor paintings and art and sculptures. One thing that I would say is that this is a small exhibit, but every single piece has purpose. It's there for a reason and I go to a lot of museums that are really big and I kind of get lost in it cuz it's so much and overwhelming and not every piece kind of like makes sense to me, but this is not the case at all here. So next up, we paid a visit to the Kigali Genocide Memorial, which is a very important stop if you are visiting Rwanda. So prior to booking this trip, I didn't really know too much about Rwanda outside of watching Hotel Rwanda in school. So I knew that when we booked this trip, this is a place that we needed to go and so many people recommended it for good reason. So as soon as you walk into the building, there is an informative video where we got to listen to the accounts of first-hand survivors and you're not allowed to take videos or pictures inside of the museum because there is some graphic content. >> So after hearing about these first-hand experiences, I was really curious like how did this even happen? And one thing that I will say is that this museum was very good about explaining how things happened as well as the process and the timeline of how Rwanda is the way that it is today. I don't want to get into too much details here because I want all of you guys to come and visit this museum yourself. It is amazing, so educational and informative about so many genocides around the world and how we can prevent them. Yes, and it is free to visit, but they do accept donations in all currencies. So this is actually a memorial because it is the reburial site of many of the victims of the genocide. It's actually home to over 250,000 bodies here. There also is a rose garden and a water fountain, but this place is a must-visit for your trip in Kigali. >> So we heard one of the best places for views in the city, especially the sunset, is at Heaven Restaurant. And let me tell you, the place did not disappoint. It had amazing views. It was slightly obstructed by like the trees and stuff, but definitely beautiful and hilly. Kigali is known as the city of a thousand hills for a reason. Yes, so they have a lot of vegetarian and vegan options. I went with the isombe, which was an African stew made with cassava leaves, and it came with rice and beans, and I am stuffed. I also got a ginger latte, and it was super ginger, very spicy. I got a traditional Rwandan stew, which is vegan, but you had the option to also add in chicken or beef, so I added the chicken, and it was so good, super hearty, and I love to eat and be full, so this is definitely the place to go. Also, they treated us to like a Japanese plum dessert that was really good. And I also got a honey hibiscus drink, which I love. So I definitely would recommend to go here, especially for sunrise or sunset, but let's keep this journey going. So from Heaven Restaurant, we actually walked to Ubumwe Hotel Restaurant. It's not a 14-minute walk on Google Maps, but it doesn't really account for all the hills that the city really has. Seriously, the city of rolling hills, okay? Lots of uphill battles for me, but we made it, and honestly, this is the best views of the city. Yeah, we should have came to Ubumwe for the sunset because you can see every single thing in the city. You see the roundabouts. I mean, we're here at night now, and just seeing it all lit up right now, this place is amazing. You know, we're here chilling. It's honestly it's a Tuesday afternoon or Tuesday night now, and there's really nobody here. So I got here, I grabbed a Fanta Fiesta, and I love that outside of the US they use real sugar in these glass bottles. Um Yes, and I got a chamomile tea, which is really great for me to end my day. All right, and just like that, we're back at our hotel room. And if you haven't noticed by now, we're not exactly nightlife people, but we did ask our taxi driver for some recommendations. So there's People Nightclub, and there's also Cadillac Nightclub. So you can check those out, those are popular. If you're not fighting for your life from jet lag like us, let us know in the comments how they are, or if you have any other recommendations, because Rwanda definitely does have a lot of nightlife. So this is supposed to be our day two footage in Rwanda, but unfortunately, our SD card got corrupted, and we don't have that footage. So this is us back at the Choose house telling you guys about our experience. Now, one thing I will say is Rwanda absolutely surprised us in the best way. And Kigali has actually surprised me to be one of my favorite cities I've visited in Africa so far. Honestly, I haven't been to a lot of places where I look around and I say, "I could live here." But Kigali might be the one city that I visited and I thought, "I could live here." There are so many different things that I felt that definitely made me feel like I could live there. Number one is majority of the people did speak English. Number two, the infrastructure was really nice. It was super clean, it's easy to get around. There is a lot of traffic, and we were able to get basically a taxi and use our driver to get throughout. >> Also, what was very important to me was that I felt pretty safe the entire time, even walking around the different neighborhoods. Yeah, we were able to walk around even late at night, and we felt completely safe the entire time. The only issue I might have had was running out of breath because it is kind of hilly, but still very gorgeous. Even going through the local markets, everyone was kind and friendly. Obviously, they are trying to sell you things, but we never felt intimidated in our time. If we knew what we know now about Rwanda and Kigali, we would have definitely spent more time than just the two days. But we planned this itinerary really just to stop in Kigali before we went to our next destination. There was also a really good woman-led coffee shop called Question Coffee that we enjoyed on our second day there, and they also had different experiences where you can actually go out into the fields, too, and see the whole process. Yeah, I really love that Kigali and Rwanda is so forward-thinking. I think it's one of the only countries in the world where it's woman-led, whether it's in the Senate or Congress, I'm not sure what it's called, but the majority of the seats are filled by women, which is astounding and basically unheard of anywhere else in the world. It's crazy that this country had such a lasting impression on us because we really didn't know much about Rwanda before, outside of the genocide that we learned about in school. The way that Rwanda was able to grow and progress is mind-boggling, and a lot of countries around the world really need to take note and learn from them. So even though our day two footage in Kigali, Rwanda is corrupted, we had an amazing time, and our time in Africa is not over yet. And while we were in Kigali, we actually met with a lot of different locals and got plenty of local recommendations from where to stay, where to eat, nightlife, other things to do that we weren't able to do, but came highly recommended. And we included all of those details inside of our Explore Rwanda travel guide, which you can find on the choose explore.com or links below. So to end our time in Rwanda, we actually took a 10-hour bus from Kigali to Uganda, and that's where the next part of this journey will continue. So thank you guys so much for checking out our channel. Please be sure to like, comment, subscribe, and you could be anywhere in the world, but you decided to be here with the Choose, and we appreciate that. So there you guys have it. Choose out.

So can Kigali, Rwanda really be this easy to visit?

videos.title: Is Rwanda Really That Different? My Honest Nigerian Perspective
videos.description:
#rwanda #kigali 

Things I Wish I Knew Before Visiting Rwanda 🇷🇼 

Welcome to Rwanda 🇷🇼

After spending time exploring Kigali and other parts of Rwanda, I realized there were so many things I wish I had known before visiting.

As a Nigerian traveling in Rwanda, I noticed several differences in culture, transportation, cleanliness, safety, cost of living, food, and daily life that completely surprised me.

In this video, I'm sharing my honest experiences, travel tips, and lessons learned to help anyone planning a trip to Rwanda.

Whether you're visiting from Nigeria, another African country, or anywhere in the world, these are the things I genuinely wish someone had told me before I arrived.

If you're planning a Rwanda trip, considering moving to Rwanda, or simply curious about life in one of Africa's fastest-growing countries, this video is for you.

Subscribe for more travel stories across Africa.

📍 Locations:
Kigali, Rwanda

💬 Question:
What's one thing you wish someone told you before visiting a new country?


things to know before visiting Rwanda
Rwanda travel tips
Rwanda travel guide
visiting Rwanda
Nigerian in Rwanda
Rwanda vs Nigeria
Kigali travel guide
Rwanda travel vlog
life in Rwanda
moving to Rwanda
travel Africa
East Africa travel

Planning a trip to Rwanda? This Rwanda travel guide covers exactly what you need to know to navigate the country safely and easily.

Whether you are a first-time visitor or planning your next adventure, this video breaks down the practical side of visiting Rwanda. We cover essential preparation steps, including how to handle visa-friendly requirements and why the country is known for its security consciousness. If you are preparing for your upcoming travel to Africa, these insights will help you feel more confident about your itinerary.

Beyond logistics, we discuss how to manage daily life on the ground. You will learn the importance of downloading offline maps before you arrive and why dressing modestly is a simple but vital cultural adjustment. We also touch on communication, confirming that English is widely understood, which simplifies your interactions throughout your stay. These Rwanda travel tips are designed to help you focus on the experience rather than the logistics of your Rwanda tourism journey.

Subscribe for weekly travel breakdowns, and comment below with which country you want to learn about next.
Planning Rwanda travel without the stress of complex visa requirements? Learn why Rwanda is a top destination for hassle-free trips.

This video is for travelers who want to explore East Africa without worrying about administrative roadblocks. We discuss how Rwanda simplifies the entry process for visitors, making it a straightforward choice for your next vacation. Whether you are planning a solo trip or a group adventure, understanding the logistics is the first step.

We also address a common question regarding Rwanda pronunciation to ensure you are confident when speaking about your travel plans. If you have been searching for visa free countries to add to your bucket list, this overview helps you decide if this destination fits your needs.

Subscribe for weekly travel breakdowns and comment below on how you pronounce Rwanda so we can settle the debate.

videos.title: 5 DAYS IN RWANDA 🇷🇼 | What They Don’t Show You on TV
videos.description:
#rwanda #kigali #africa




This is my full Rwanda experience in one video — 5 days of exploring Kigali, trying Rwandan food, learning the culture, meeting people, visiting markets, and discovering why Rwanda is one of Africa’s most admired countries.

From the airport, to accommodation, to the clean streets and the food — this is the REAL Rwanda, unfiltered and honest.

Perfect for anyone planning a trip or just curious about what Kigali is truly like.



5 days in Rwanda, Rwanda travel guide, Kigali full vlog, Rwanda tourism, Africa travel vlog series, best things to do in Kigali, Ejeomah Rwanda vlog




#Rwanda #Kigali #TravelAfrica #RwandaVlog #AfricaTravel #Ejeomah
